And when it comes to trying to implement anything, especially in intervention, it's all about finding the right tool for the job. And sometimes that tool is natural. Sometimes that tool is synthetic. Sometimes that tool is, you don't know, you just know it works. And so having a bias like this is actually like, goes way back into like fallacy stuff that I learned about in grade school. And this is like, this is called the naturalistic fallacy. And this is making the assumption that just because it came out of the ground means it's better. And I can tell you that nature is metal. Or what, I think that's the most popular one I get asked about. Okay, so like, you eat breakfast at nine and you eat lunch at noon and you eat dinner at five. So you have breakfast, lunch and dinner like every other normal human and call it intermittent fasting. Okay. Hello. Again, tool for the job. Like, there's absolutely nothing magic about this. If there are ways that you can implement a nutrition protocol that make a person's life easier, absolutely. But in the context of something like this, if you're going to implement an intervention that makes a person's life miserable, it's not like what grandma told you about medicine. I want to discuss the ketogenic diet a little bit. I'm not going to talk about like, oh, the ratio of oxaloacetate and the tricarboxylic acid cycle was off. And so I'm not going to talk about that. What I'm going to talk about is that when people talk about starvation mode, an actual ketogenic diet, that's what starvation mode is. Okay. If you don't get enough carbohydrate, if you don't get enough food, your blood sugar and insulin starts to go. And it gets so low that the brain is like, holy crap, I'm going to shut down if I don't get some grub. And what ends up happening is that you end up releasing body fat. You release that body fat and your liver converts that to ketones. So your brain doesn't shut off. That's starvation mode. Just so you know, like when you talk about starvation mode or think about starvation mode, starve a person. Take the biochemical analysis and that's what happens. Your brain is like, okay, we got nothing. What can we do? We got to switch the diesel. Okay. That's what happens. And so it would make perfect sense that like, okay, I'm in ketosis. I don't know what it means, but like, sounds cool. And I also know that it's somehow connected to body fat. Like, okay, I eat more fat. I burn more fat makes perfect sense. And like, okay, 99% right 100% wrong. And so yeah, it makes perfect sense. It also makes perfect sense that like the planets revolve around the sun in a circle and then Kepler's like screw you guys. It's an oval. But basically it makes perfect sense. Like, okay, I'm going to eat a bunch of fat, cut out my carbohydrate, you know, screw the water dump. Everybody knows about that stuff. I'm burning body fat now. Okay. And so what a lot of confusion ends up happening is that 100% of the fat you eat gets stored basically. And so the fact that you're burning is the fat that you consumed yesterday, the day before, two months before. Whatever the turnover for adipose tissue is, I'm actually not entirely sure for complete turnover. But the point is that, yeah, all the fat you burn is body fat. It has to be processed and packaged and delivered and stored before you can mobilize it and burn it again. And so, okay, you're on a ketogenic diet. You're burning body fat. I mean, you're burning body fat when you're not in a ketogenic diet either. That's okay. And so you stop losing water weight and you're like, man, how do I keep losing fat on this ketogenic diet where I'm supposed to be burning body fat. I'm supposed to be burning body fat all the time. When you're burning body fat all the time anyway. And so the natural really, if you're a theological person, it's okay. I decrease the amount of fat I consume to make sure I lose body fat because you already cut out of the carbohydrates, right? You're eating like 10 grams of twigs and tuna and kale, whatever else people eat on the ketogenic diet. And you lower your fat. Lo and behold, your body fat starts to come off. Okay. And so food processing is like... Almost everybody in here is alive because of food processing. Okay. Everything's on a continuum, so there's a psychological binary and a biological continuum, okay? It's not are you diabetic or are you not diabetic, it's how diabetic are you, okay? Okay? Cravings are really a feeling versus a biological signal. And so what food does is it stabilizes your mood. Okay? And so burn PM acts as an anxiolytic. Otherwise you don't get the full benefit of all three branch chain amino acids versus drinking it throughout the day. Is that true? Well, the short answer is yes. But the question for the question sake is that it's okay to consume it if you like the taste throughout the day. Like that's the reality. But you don't get the benefits of the three amino acids if you drink it throughout the day. Well, it's considered a nutrient source. So like basically in terms of turning on protein synthesis and all that sort of stuff, it needs to be a full meal. And so there are all like the essential amino acids in there will trigger some sort of synthesis, but it's still dependent on you eating actual food. So like basically you are correct. You're not getting benefits by having tiny trickles of amino acids in your bloodstream throughout the day because it's just a minuscule amount over time. So in the context of drinking it all day because you like it and the way it tastes and it keeps you hydrated because there's other stuff in there besides aminos, I'm all for it. But you're right. Drinking seven grams of amino acids over an eight hour period is not going to cause any magical muscle growth to happen. You are correct. Thank you. Right. Right.
